title_en: Taharah in Al-Insaf: Preferred Hanbali Purification Positions
title_ar: الطهارة في الإنصاف: المذهب الحنبلي المعتمد
content_en:
Al-Mardawi's treatment of taharah in Al-Insaf presents the preferred (rajih) Hanbali positions on purification law by surveying the diversity of transmitted opinions within the school and identifying which ones the school's authorities have most consistently supported.

On the question of water categories, al-Mardawi identifies the preferred Hanbali positions on which types of water are pure and purifying. The Hanbali school, like the Shafi'i school, distinguishes between pure water that purifies (tahir mutahhir), pure water that does not purify (tahir ghayr mutahhir — principally used water), and impure water (najis). Al-Mardawi surveys the Hanbali opinions on used water, noting that the preferred position is that water used in a wudu' or ghusl loses its purifying capacity even if its qualities have not changed — consistent with the Shafi'i position and stricter than the Hanafi school.

The Hanbali school's position on small quantities of water contaminated by impurity is addressed in Al-Insaf. The school holds that water less than two qullahs (approximately 270 liters, or a pool roughly 60 cm square) in volume becomes impure if any physical impurity falls into it — regardless of whether its qualities change. Al-Mardawi identifies this as the rajih Hanbali position based on the prophetic report about two qullahs, while noting that some Hanbali scholars held the quality-change criterion as the operative standard. This is one of the most significant inter-school differences in water law.

For wudu', al-Mardawi identifies the preferred Hanbali positions on the obligatory elements. The Hanbali school requires: washing the face (including the area from hairline to chin and ear to ear, with the mouth and nose as obligatory components of face-washing); washing the arms to and including the elbows; wiping the entire head (including the ears); and washing the feet to and including the ankles. The requirement to wipe the entire head — not merely part of it — is shared with the Maliki school and differs from the Shafi'i school (any amount) and the Hanafi school (one quarter).

The Hanbali school's requirement that the mouth and nose be washed as part of washing the face in wudu' is a distinctive position shared with the Hanafi school (which also requires this in ghusl). Al-Mardawi identifies this as the rajih Hanbali position, noting that it is grounded in the prophetic hadith commanding that the nostrils be cleansed during wudu' and in the Hanbali interpretation of the verse on ablution.

On the permission to wipe over socks (mash ala al-khuffayn), al-Mardawi identifies the Hanbali positions on the conditions: the socks must cover the ankles, must be put on in a state of purity, and the time limits apply (one day-night for residents, three for travelers). The Hanbali school is distinctive in permitting the wiping of socks made of any material — not only leather — provided they are sturdy enough to walk in, based on authenticated hadith that do not restrict the permission to leather specifically.

Tayammum in Al-Insaf is addressed with the Hanbali requirement that the clean earth be struck twice — once for the face and once for the hands to the wrists (not the elbows, as the Hanafi school holds). The preferred Hanbali position on the type of surface valid for tayammum is that it must be clean earth or what has 'the qualities of earth' — a position that excludes some surfaces that other schools would permit.

Al-Mardawi's systematic presentation of preferred Hanbali positions in taharah gives students a reliable map of the school's consensus positions — the result of his exhaustive survey of the school's transmitted literature translated into clear, actionable legal guidance.
